coredata.dc:title: β-Cyclodextrin anchored neem carbon dots for enhanced electrochemical sensing performance of an anticancer drug, lapatinib via host-guest inclusion
coredata.dc:creator: Mathad, Ayyapayya S
Seetharamappa, J.
Kalanur, Shankara S
coredata.dc:description: By utilizing the merits of β-Cyclodextrin and neem carbon dots (C-dots), a simple, eco-friendly, and sensitive electrode material, β-CD functionalized neem carbon dots modified glassy carbon electrode (β-CD@C-dots/GCE) was developed for electrochemical determination of an anticancer drug, lapatinib (LAP). A green and cost-effective approach was adopted for the synthesis of neem carbon dots (C-dots) via the one-pot hydrothermal treatment of leaf extracts of neem (Azadirachta indica). The β-CD was integrated onto C-dots through hydrogen bonding. XRD, FTIR, TEM, fluorescence, UV–Vis, TGA, electrochemical impedance, and cyclic voltammetric studies confirmed the formation of nanocomposite, β-CD@C-dots and used as an electrode material for sensing application. Factors affecting the activity of the sensing interface were investigated by cyclic voltammetry and optimized for sensitive performance. The proposed sensor has displayed the analyte capturing ability (via host-guest inclusion complex formation) as evident from the accumulation of more LAP on the electrode surface. The peak current of LAP was observed to be enhanced by about 30-fold at the modified electrode compared to that at the bare electrode. LAP exhibited three anodic peaks at 0.55 V (Ep, a3), 0.90 V (Ep, a1), and 1.12 V (Ep, a2), and one cathodic peak at 0.47 V (Ep, c1) in phosphate buffer of pH 2.5. Electrode processes were realized under adsorption controlled for peak a1 and diffusion–adsorption mixed controlled process for peak a2, respectively. The synergistic effect of β-CD and C-dots electrode material exhibited a wide linear relationship between the oxidation peak current (peak a1) and concentration of LAP in the range of 1.99 × 10−8 − 8.59 × 10−6 mol L−1, 0.99 × 10−8 − 6.17 × 10−6 mol L−1 and 4.99 × 10−8 − 6.17 × 10−6 mol L−1 for differential pulse voltammetric, square wave voltammetric and linear sweep voltammetric methods, respectively. The lowest LOD value of 1.81 × 10−10 mol L−1 was achieved with the SWV method. The proposed sensor demonstrated satisfactory applicability for the determination of LAP in pharmaceutical formulations and urine samples. Thus, β-CD@C-dots/GCE is a promising and high-performing electrochemical sensor for the determination of LAP.

head.ce:title: β-Cyclodextrin anchored neem carbon dots for enhanced electrochemical sensing performance of an anticancer drug, lapatinib via host-guest inclusion

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: By utilizing the merits of β-Cyclodextrin and neem carbon dots (C-dots), a simple, eco-friendly, and sensitive electrode material, β-CD functionalized neem carbon dots modified glassy carbon electrode (β-CD@C-dots/GCE) was developed for electrochemical determination of an anticancer drug, lapatinib (LAP). A green and cost-effective approach was adopted for the synthesis of neem carbon dots (C-dots) via the one-pot hydrothermal treatment of leaf extracts of neem (Azadirachta indica). The β-CD was integrated onto C-dots through hydrogen bonding. XRD, FTIR, TEM, fluorescence, UV–Vis, TGA, electrochemical impedance, and cyclic voltammetric studies confirmed the formation of nanocomposite, β-CD@C-dots and used as an electrode material for sensing application. Factors affecting the activity of the sensing interface were investigated by cyclic voltammetry and optimized for sensitive performance. The proposed sensor has displayed the analyte capturing ability (via host-guest inclusion complex formation) as evident from the accumulation of more LAP on the electrode surface. The peak current of LAP was observed to be enhanced by about 30-fold at the modified electrode compared to that at the bare electrode. LAP exhibited three anodic peaks at 0.55 V (Ep, a3), 0.90 V (Ep, a1), and 1.12 V (Ep, a2), and one cathodic peak at 0.47 V (Ep, c1) in phosphate buffer of pH 2.5. Electrode processes were realized under adsorption controlled for peak a1 and diffusion–adsorption mixed controlled process for peak a2, respectively. The synergistic effect of β-CD and C-dots electrode material exhibited a wide linear relationship between the oxidation peak current (peak a1) and concentration of LAP in the range of 1.99 × 10−8 − 8.59 × 10−6 mol L−1, 0.99 × 10−8 − 6.17 × 10−6 mol L−1 and 4.99 × 10−8 − 6.17 × 10−6 mol L−1 for differential pulse voltammetric, square wave voltammetric and linear sweep voltammetric methods, respectively. The lowest LOD value of 1.81 × 10−10 mol L−1 was achieved with the SWV method. The proposed sensor demonstrated satisfactory applicability for the determination of LAP in pharmaceutical formulations and urine samples. Thus, β-CD@C-dots/GCE is a promising and high-performing electrochemical sensor for the determination of LAP.

ce:section.ce:para: Breast cancer is the most often diagnosed and major cause of mortality in women globally with over a million cases, yearly. In about 25–30% of patients, breast cancer exhibits an amplification and/or overexpression of the receptor tyrosine kinase HER2 which is associated with a poor prognosis for patient survival [1]. The targeted anti-cancer drug, lapatinib (LAP) is an orally active small molecule that belongs to a family of tyrosine kinase inhibitors (TKIs). This inhibitor is known to suppress cell proliferation by blocking the intracellular domain of twooncogenes, EGFR (epidermal growth factor receptor; ErbB1) and HER2/neu (human EGFR type 2; ErbB2) [2]. Therefore, it is approved for safe and effective targeted therapy against women's breast cancer and other solid tumors [3]. Therapeutic strategies involve blocking kinase-substrate interaction, inhibiting receptor signal processes by reacting with ATP binding site and blocking extracellular tyrosine kinase receptors on cancer cells, preventing self-phosphorylationand subsequent proliferative signaling [4,5].

Literature reports several analytical methods for the quantification of lapatinib in biological matrices and dosage forms including high-performance liquid chromatography[6,7], LC-MS-MS[8–11], LC-MS[12], UPLC/MS-MS [13]. The reported methods have some inherent disadvantages such as time-consuming, use of toxic chemicals, involving elaborate extraction process and purification steps or online sample extraction, and use of costly solvents. Therefore, an alternative method for sensitive determination of LAP is necessary. Alternatively, electrochemical methods have proven to be the best of all other analytical techniques due to their high sensitivity, low detection limits, inexpensive equipment, real-time detection without any separation processes, fewer reagents consumption, and simple surface modification of electrodes.

Burcu Dogan-Topal et al [14] have examined the electro-oxidation of LAP and its interaction with dsDNA at glassy carbon electrode(GCE) while, Begum Evranos Aksoz et al,[15] have used GCE for the quantification of LAP in the concentration range of 2.0 × 10−8 to 1.0 × 10−6 mol L−1 with a limit of detection(LOD) of 5.71 nM. To the best of our knowledge, only one electroanalytical method [10] is reported for the quantification of LAP at GCE. To develop an electrochemical method for the assay of LAP at the nanomolar level, we have fabricated a simple, sensitive and eco-friendly electrochemical sensing platform based on β-CD@C-dots film on GCE.

Carbon nanodots (C-dots), novel zero-dimensional carbon nanoparticles have been recognized as discrete, quasi-spherical nanocrystals with a size less than 10 nm. They generally possess a graphite structure or amorphous sp2 conjugated carbon core and carbonaceous surfaces that are made up of oxygen-based moieties. As a new class of carbon nanomaterial, fluorescent C-dots have attracted considerable attention owing to their intriguing properties such as green synthesis, chemical inertness, tunable luminescence, low cost, high solubility, excellent photochemical and physico-chemical stabilities, versatile surface modification and functionalization, biocompatibility, the abundance of raw materials and low cytotoxicity. As a green substitute for toxic metal quantum dots, C-dots have promising applications in various fields including electrocatalysis[16], biosensing [17], bioimaging[18], photocatalysis[19], fluorescence/ electrochemical sensors[20], optoelectronic and energy devices[21], drug delivery [22], and so on. Due to their large chemically functionalizable surface, efficient catalytic activity[23], and remarkable conductivity, they can be an ideal electrode material for electrochemical sensor applications. However, the use of C-dots by combining with other materials has provided more interspaces and pathways for both volume change and ions transfer and acted as excellent conductive agents to enhance the conductivity and electrochemical performance[24]. Despite having outstanding electronic properties, limited research is focused on the usage of C-dots in the analytical field till recently. However, C-dots, N or S doped C-dots, and their nanocomposites based electrochemical sensors were successfully used for the electrochemical analysis of organic molecules such as etoposide[25], dopamine[26–28], curcumin[29], L-cysteine[30], flutamide and nitrofurantoin[31], hydrogen peroxide and paracetamol[32], uric acid[33], hydroquinone and catechol[34], adrenaline[35], rilpivirine[20], patulin[36], NADH [37], olanzapine[38] and TNT[39]. The results of these studies demonstrated that C-dots facilitated the electron transfer and acted as catalysts.

Several top-down and bottom-up methods have been reported for the production of C-dots. Top-down methods include arc discharge [40], laser ablation[41], and electrochemical[42] and acidic oxidation[43,44]. Though the above conventional methods were successfully applied for the production of C-dots, unfortunately, they suffered from serious drawbacks such as the use of toxic reagents /organic solvents as precursors, tedious process, and special and sophisticated instrumental set-up. While, bottom-up approaches like carbonization of glucose, citric acid, glycerol, glycol, ascorbic acid, sucrose, etc., have considerable attention for the synthesis of fluorescent C-dots [45–48].

Nevertheless, most of the reported routes involve the use of strong acids, multistep synthesis, as well as post-treatments with a surface, and usage of passivated agents (improves luminescence property and water solubility). These conventional approaches do not meet the requirements of green chemistry as they are not sustainable either in synthetic techniques or, in raw materials. Therefore, the development of the “greener” methodology using inexpensive nontoxic carbon precursors and environmentally friendly solvents is extremely important in the synthesis of carbon dots for safe application. Over the past few years, “hydrothermal carbonization” has been recognized as an ecofriendly, and soft chemical synthetic route for the production of C-dots in aqueous media using various cheap and bio-waste materials as precursors such as lotus root[49], green tea[50], potatoes[51], egg[52], orange juice[53], apple juice [54] coriander leaves[55], and pepper[56], etc. Neem leaves have medicinal properties such as antimicrobial, antibacterial, antifungal, etc., Moreover, they are cheap and green sources of carbon for the synthesis of C-dots. Given the green chemistry approach, it is worthwhile to develop a one-pot sustainable synthetic route to prepare fluorescent neem quantum carbon dots (C-dot) for sensor applications.

β-Cyclodextrin (β-CD), ‘α’ cyclic oligosaccharide that contains seven D (+)-glucose units, presents a toroidal form with an outer hydrophilic environment and inner hydrophobic cavity[57]. This unique configuration of β-CD makes it the most suitable host to bind selectively with hydrophobic organic molecules like lapatinib into its cavity. This accommodation forms stable guest-host inclusion complexes or nanostructured supermolecular assemblies, through noncovalent interactions such as hydrophobic, electrostatic, van der Waals interactions, or hydrogen bonding. Whereas, the peripheral of β-CD could make it as functional molecules to increase the dispersibility of functional materials in solvents, which is very significant for high sensitivity and stability of sensors. Because of their remarkable host-guest supramolecular recognition capability, biocompatible, and non-toxic nature, β-CD has been extensively evaluated for modification of electrodes in selective detection of electroactive molecules. The interaction between the hydrophobic cavity of β-CD and hydrophobic LAP facilitated the accumulation of more LAP molecules on the electrode surface and in turn, enhanced the electrochemical sensing applications [58–61]. The interfacial architecture and enhanced electrochemical activity of β-CDs based carbon nanomaterials (CNT, graphene, carbon nanohorns, and graphene quantum dots) modified electrodes after incorporating β-CDs on the surface of carbon nanomaterials, and the resulting recognition effects have been demonstrated [39,62–64]. The β-CD molecules could be anchored to the surface of C-dots substrate by non-covalent and covalent reactions. The resulting nanohybrid β-CD@C-dots could exhibit the synergistic effect of both C-dots and β-CD. It is reported that “the formation of LAP–β-CD inclusion complex is thermodynamically favorable with the energy formation of −10.7 kcal/mol[65], suggesting the feasibility of β-CD-based electrochemical selective sensing of LAP molecule. Therefore, the β-CD appended C-dots nanohybrid could display fascinating electrocatalytic properties and excellent enrichment and supramolecular recognition capabilities towards LAP.

LAP and β-Cyclodextrin were purchased from Sigma Aldrich, USA, and used without further purification. The neem (Azadirachta indica) leaves were collected from the botanical garden area of Karnatak University, Dharwad, Karnataka, India. A stock solution of 1 mM LAP was prepared by dissolving an appropriate amount of LAP in methanol and stored in the refrigerator at 4 0C. Solutions of different working concentrations of LAP were obtained by suitable dilution with the selected supporting electrolyte (0.1 M phosphate buffer). The methanol ratio (20% (v/v) was kept constant in each working solution. All other chemicals used were of analytical grade and used as received. All other solutions were prepared in milli-pore water.

A CHI6134E electrochemical analyzer (CH Instruments Ltd. Co., USA), with a conventional three-electrode cell, was employed for electrochemical measurements. Differential pulse voltammetric (DPV) studies were performed by using the differential scan rate from 0.6 to 1.6 V with a pulse width of 50 ms, pulse amplitude of 50 mV, and a scan rate of 100 mV s−1. For square wave voltammetry (SWV), pulse amplitude of 25.05 mV, a potential increment of 4.95 mV, and a scan rate of 74.2 mV s−1 were used.

The X-ray diffraction patterns of samples were recorded on an XRD Bruker AXS D8 diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (1.5406 A0). Electrochemical impedance measurements were carried out by applying a sinusoidal signal of amplitude 10 mV and the frequency in the range of 10 kHz to 0.1 Hz. Raman and FTIR spectral data were recorded on an NXR-FT-Raman, Nicolet, USA, and Nicolet 6700, Nicolet, USA, respectively. Surface morphologies were studied using a JEOL Model JSM-6390LV scanning electron microscope (SEM). Absorption spectra were recorded on an AU-2701 UV–Vis double beam spectrophotometer (India). Transmission electron microscopic (TEM) images were obtained from a TEM, JEOL 2010F.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of carbon nanodots (C-dots) and β-CD@C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: C-dots were prepared by the hydrothermal carbonization method by following the reported method with a slight modification [66]. Briefly, 15 g of fresh leaves of neem were crushed into the fine powder and dissolved in 150 mL of distilled water, and sonicated for 15 min. The resulting suspension was transferred into a 250 mL of Teflon-lined autoclave and heated in the oven at 150 °C for 4 h. After the completion of the reaction, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature to obtain a brown carbonaceous solution. It was filtered through a 0.45 µm filter to remove large size particles and washed with dichloromethane to eliminate unreacted organic moieties. The collected aqueous solution was centrifuged at 15000 rpm for 15 min to remove the larger size particles. The supernatant of the centrifuged solution was collected and further dialyzed for 24 h against double distilled water. Finally, pure and clear brown colored C-dots were obtained and were stored at 4 °C for further use.

The aqueous solution of β-CD was prepared by dispersing β-CD (2 mg/mL) into distilled water and subjected to sonication for 15 min. Then, 20 mg of C-dots was mixed with β-CD solution and the mixture was sonicated for 3 h. The resulting suspension was centrifuged and washed with distilled water thrice to eliminate free β-CD. Finally, the homogeneous β-CD@C-dots suspension (1 mg mL−1) was prepared in Millipore water by ultra-sonication for 30 min.

The modified electrode was prepared by a simple drop-casting method. Before each electrochemical experiment, the GCE was carefully polished with 0.05 μm aluminum oxide slurry on a polishing cloth pad. After polishing, it was rinsed thoroughly with double distilled water. Following this mechanical treatment, the electrode was allowed to dry in the air. To obtain a modified GCE, 6 μL of β-CD@C-dots suspension was dropped on the GCE surface. The electrode was then dried under an IR lamp to achieve the desired electrode of β-CD@C-dots/GCE.

Five tablets of LAP (each containing 250.0 mg LAP) were weighed and finely pulverized in a mortar. The required amount of homogenized tablets powder for the preparation of 1 mM stock solution of LAP, was weighed and dissolved in 50 mL of methanol. It was then subjected to sonication for 30 min. Suitable working solutions were prepared by proper dilution with the supporting electrolyte and analyzed by the SWV method. The quantity of LAP in the tablet was assessed using the regression equation obtained from the calibration graph.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of LAP in human urine samples
ce:section.ce:para: Analyte fortified urine stock solution was prepared by adding 1 mL (2 mM) LAP solution to 1 mL drug-free human urine sample obtained from healthy volunteers. A suitable aliquot of analyte fortified urine was diluted with a phosphate buffer solution of pH 2.5. It was then transferred into the electrochemical cell to record the voltammograms from the SWV method at β-CD@C-dots/GCE. Further, recovery of LAP was determined from the calibration plot.

The size distribution, morphology, and the chemical composition of prepared nanocomposite β-CD/C-Dots were characterized. The size and morphology of the prepared C-dots were characterized by a transmission electron microscope (TEM). The TEM micrograph (Fig. 1
A) indicated the spherical shape of C-dots with a narrow size distribution ranging between 1.5 nm and 6.0 nm. The average diameter of C-dots was found to be 3.3 nm ( Fig. 1 B), whereas the average size of β-CD/C-dots increased to 5.6 nm ( Fig. 1 D) owing to the conjugation of β-CD. XRD is useful to get insights into the nanostructure of prepared materials. The XRD pattern of C-dots as depicted in Fig. 1 E displayed a broad peak (002 plane) centered at 2θ = 24.5° corresponding to interlayer spacing distance (dhkl) of 3.63 Å, which is similar to the lattice spacing in graphite [66]. This revealed the presence of highly disordered graphitic carbon in C-dots nanostructure, which is in turn responsible for the conductivity of C-dots.

The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded to characterize the surface functional groups of C-dots and β-CD@C-dots composites. The FTIR spectrum of C-dots shown in Fig. 1 F showed broad absorption bands at 3100–3500 cm−1 due to the stretching vibrations of N-H and O-H. The peaks at 2924 and 2852 cm−1 were assigned to C-H stretching vibrations. The bands at 1736, 1650, and 1282 cm−1 were attributed to C = O, C = C, and C-O/C-N stretching vibrations respectively[67]. The FTIR spectrum of β-CD@C-dots showed representative β-CD absorption bands of the asymmetric glycosidic stretching vibrations(C-O-C) at 1150 cm−1, C-H/O-H bending vibrations at 1410 cm−1, the coupled stretching vibration of C-C and C-O at 1032 cm−1 and CH2 stretching vibrations at 2930 cm−1 [27] demonstrating that the β-CDs were grafted on C-dots[68,69]. Since both C-dots and β-CD contained abundant hydroxyl (–OH) groups, the self-assembly interaction mechanism between β-CD and C-dots was facilitated via strong intermolecular hydrogen bonding.

Raman spectrum (Fig. 2
A) of C-dots contained two distinct peaks at 1345 cm−1 and 1612 cm−1 which are ascribed to disorder-induced D and graphitic-G bands, respectively[70]. The relative intensity of disordered D and crystalline G bands (ID/IG) is a measure of the extent of the disorder nature. The ratio (ID/IG) was calculated to be 1.21 for C-dots indicating the presence of defects in the structure of carbonaceous material caused by the existence of oxygenated functional groups [71]. UV–Vis absorption ( Fig. 2 B) and fluorescence ( Fig. 3
A) spectra of C-dots were recorded to evaluate the optical properties. The C-dots exhibited two notable peaks in the UV–vis absorption spectrum. The absorption band observed in UV region around 270 nm was the characteristic π → π* transition of aromatic –C–C–, and –C = C–bonds in the sp2 hybridized domain of graphitic core while the band at 338 nm was attributed to n → π* transition of C–N, –C = O or –C–OH bonds in the sp3 hybridized domains that were originated from amine (–NH2) or carboxyl (–COOH) groups which existed on C-dots surface. The decreased absorption intensity and a slight red shift in λmax were noticed when β-CD was functionalized on C-dots surface. These results indicated that the β-CD was effectively conjugated on C-dots surface and blocked the active groups of C-dots. Further, fluorescence spectra of C-dots were recorded. The solution of C-dots exhibited excitation-wavelength-dependent photoluminescence (FL) properties and showed emission peaks ranging from 530 nm to 610 nm upon excitation from 260 nm to 500 nm ( Fig. 3 A). Maximum fluorescence intensity was noticed at 540 nm upon excitation at 420 nm. Moreover, the emission wavelengths show a red-shift with the increase in wavelength. The plausible reasons for this behavior were the presence of different sizes of C-dots and the distribution of surface emissive traps of C-dots [42].

The presence of β-CD on C-dots was evaluated by TGA as shown in Fig. 3 B. The C-dots displayed an obvious mass loss at ∼ 200 °C, which was due to the pyrolysis of the oxygen-containing functional groups. The loss in mass reached about 51.0 wt% at ∼ 600 °C. The β-CD@C-dots showed an abrupt mass loss at ∼ 260 °C due to the decomposition of β-CD [72,73]. The mass loss reached about 69.0 wt% when the temperature attained 600 °C. The β-CD that was grafted on C-dots was observed to be 18.0 wt%.

Heterogeneous electron transfer kinetics and electrochemical performance of bare GCE and the modified electrodes were investigated with the aid of cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). Fig. S2
A displays the cyclic voltammograms of 1 mM K4[Fe(CN6)] in 1 M KCl at bare GCE, β-CD/GCE, C-dots/GCE, and C-dots/β-CD/GCE with a scan rate of 0.1 V s−1. The redox probe exhibited a pair of well-defined reversible redox peaks at bare GCE (Ip ≈ 15.7 μA). Upon the modification of GC surface with β-CD, the redox peak currents were observed to be decreased (Ip≈ 9.6 μA) due to the inferior conductivity of β-CD[74]. However, the enhanced electrochemical response was observed at C-dots/GCE (Ip≈ 28.7 μA), This was attributed to excellent conductivity and the larger surface area of C-dots/GCE that accelerated the electron transfer. After coating the film of the β-CD on the C-dots/GCE surface, the current response decreased slightly (Ip≈ 24.3 μA) due to increased electron transfer resistance. The corresponding CV results are tabulated in Table 1 . Further, the surface area of β-CD@C-dots/GCE, C-dots/GCE, and bare GCE was calculated by cyclic voltammetry using Randles-Sevcik equation given below [75],

where Ip is the peak current, n is the number of electrons transferred, A is the surface area of the electrode, D is the diffusion coefficient, υ is the scan rate and C is the concentration of the redox probe K3[Fe(CN)6]. Using the slope of the plot of Ip vs. υ1/2, the electrochemically active surface area of β-CD@C-dots/GCE, C-dots/GCE, β-CD/GCE, and bare GCE was calculated to be 0.627, 0.310, 0.097, and 0.058 cm2 , respectively. The β-CD@C-dots material exhibited a larger effective surface area that promoted a rapid charge transfer process, hence exhibiting an enhanced current response at β-CD@C-dots/GCE.

EIS is a powerful tool to investigate the electrode surface-dependent charge transfer process. In EIS, the impedance can be represented by the following equation:

where Z′(ω) is the ac-resistance, Z″(ω) is the reactance and equal to − 1/ωC), j is the imaginary unit, ω = 2πf, is the angular frequency and f is the ac-frequency. Fig. S2
B shows the Nyquist plots for bare GCE, β-CD/GCE, C-dots/GCE, and β-CD@C-dots/GCE for 1 mM K4[Fe(CN)6] as a redox probe in 1 M KCl aqueous solution. The Nyquist plot consists of a capacitive loop (semicircle part) in a higher frequency region and a linear part in a lower frequency region. The semicircle is associated with an electron transfer limited process at the electrode surface and the linear portion corresponds to the diffusion process. The value of the diameter of the semicircle reflects the interfacial charge transfer resistance (Rct)[76] which controls the charge transfer kinetics of the redox probe, K4[Fe(CN)6] at the electrode interface[77]. Based on the simulation of the suitable equivalent circuit, the Rct of bare and modified electrodes was calculated and illustrated in the inset of Fig. S2
B. The values of electrical components (Rs, Rct, CPE, and W) of equivalent circuits for each electrode are summarized in Table 1. The semicircle diameter of β-CD/GCE (Rct = 917.6 Ω) was observed to be broader than that of bare GCE (Rct = 750.4 Ω) indicating poor electron transfer ability of β-CD. The C-dots/GCE (Rct = 152.8 Ω) showed much lower impedance to the electron transfer process compared to that of bare GCE (313.7 Ω) indicating that C-dots are highly conductive and can effectively accelerate the electron transfer. However, the β-CD/C-dots/GCE exhibits a lower Rct owing to the presence of C-dots, implying that β-CD/C-dots/GCE has excellent conductivity. The reported EIS results corroborated the preceding CV results and both of them confirmed that the sensing interface was successfully achieved.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical behavior of LAP at different electrodes
ce:section.ce:para:
To assess the detailed electrochemical oxidation behavior of LAP and for the assay of LAP, cyclic voltammograms of 10 μM LAP solution in 0.1 M phosphate buffer of pH 2.5 were recorded at β-CD@C-dots/GCE (a), C-dots/GCE (b), and bare GCE (c) (shown in Fig. 4
A). LAP exhibited two anodic peaks at Ep = 0.91 V (peak a1), and at 1.12 V (peak a2) during the 1st anodic sweep from 0 to 1.5 V. No reduction peaks corresponding to either peak a1 or peak a2 were observed on reversing the scan direction indicating the irreversible nature of peak a1 and peak a2. Only one cathodic peak was noticed at 0.50 V (Peak c1). In the forward sweep of the 2nd cycle, a new wave appeared on the anodic end at 0.55 V (Peak a3) which was not present in the first scan. But, it appeared in the second and successive scans. In successive cycles, the two anodic peak currents decreased. As evident from Fig. 4 A, the pair of redox peaks (Peak a3/c1) was found to be dependent on both anodic peaks a1 and a2. The dependence of the cathodic peak c1 was verified by performing CV scans in two different working potential window ranges. As evident from Fig. 4 B, during the potential sweep from 0 to 0.95 V (at the end of the first peak), peak c1 was observed at 0.50 V. When the initial potential was 0.95 V (starting at the beginning of the second peak) the peak c1 was seen again at the same potential (0.50 V) indicating the dependency of peak c1 on both a1 and a2. This might be due to the electro-oxidation product of LAP that was electrochemically active and involved in an electrochemical redox reaction.

Furthermore, modification of GCE with C-dots led to remarkable enhancement (∼16-fold) in the anodic peak current. This was due to the presence of conductive C-dots and increased electro-active surface area that accelerated the electron transfer. In addition, the presence of β-CD on C-dots promoted a synergistic effect between C-dots and itself resulting in further amplification of electrochemical signals at β-CD@C-dots/GCE (∼30-fold higher than that at bare GCE) as compared to that at C-dots/GCE. This could be caused by the high supramolecular recognition capability of β-CD that formed an inclusion complex with LAP. The non-covalent interaction between the hydrophobic cavity of β-CD and hydrophobic LAP resulted in the enrichment of a greater amount of LAP molecules on the surface of β-CD@C-dots/GCE. This tendency of adsorbing more LAP molecules on the electrode surface resulted in enhanced detection capacity and consequently the sensitivity of the electrode.

The cyclic voltammetric technique was applied as a diagnostic tool to extract information regarding electrochemical oxidation of LAP at β-CD@C-dots/GCE in different supporting electrolytes. Hence, acetate buffer, H2SO4, phosphate buffer, and Britton-Robinson buffer solutions were examined as electrolytes. Considering the peak shape, peak potential, and peak current, phosphate buffer solution (PBS) was found to be the best-supporting electrolyte for the oxidation of LAP. So, we used PBS as an electrolyte in the present study for further electrochemical investigations. The electrochemical responses of 10 μM LAP at β-CD@C-dots/GCE performed in PBS of different pH (2.5–8) by CV are shown in Fig. S3
A. In basic solution (beyond pH 8) no oxidation peak was observed but peak a2 disappeared above pH 7.0. The best results were achieved with PBS of pH 2.5 suggesting that the electrode reaction was more favorable at pH 2.5. It could be seen from Fig. S3
A, that oxidation peak potentials of LAP were shifted to negative end, and peak current (Ip) decreased gradually with an increase in pH (2.5. − 8.0), indicating the involvement of protons in the current-limiting electrode reaction. Further, the plot of Ep vs. pH ( Fig. S3
B) revealed two different linear plots for peak a1 and one linear plot for peak a2, which are described by the following regression equations:

The obtained slope values for both peaks are close to the theoretical value of 59 mV per pH unit, indicating the participation of electrons and protons in the ratio of 1:1 in electro-oxidation reaction on β-CD@C-dots/GCE surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Scan rate studies: The relationship between peak current and scan rate
ce:section.ce:para:
Cyclic voltammetry with repetitive cycles at different scan rates (υ) provides a simple and fast approach to get insights into reaction mechanisms and kinetics. Fig. 5
A shows cyclic voltammograms of 10 μM LAP solutions at β-CD@C-dots/GCE in the scan rate range of 10–300 mV s−1. The oxidation peak potentials for both peaks were shifted towards a more positive potential end with an increase in the scan rate. A linear relationship was observed between the peak current and square root of scan rate (υ1/2) for peak a1, while for peak a2 linearity was noticed between peak current and scan rate (υ) as shown below:

Further, slope values of ∼ 1 and 0.802 were observed for the linearity plot of log ipa1 vs. log υ and log ipa2 vs. log υ (Fig. 5 B) respectively. These results revealed that the electrode processes were realized under adsorption controlled for peak a1, and diffusion–adsorption mixed controlled process for peak a2. The corresponding linear regression equations are shown below:
log

= 0.9467 l o g υ - 3.6863 ,
R

= 0.8024 l o g υ - 3.8567 ,

To evaluate electrochemical kinetic parameters viz., electron transfer coefficient (α), number of electrons transferred (n), and electron transfer rate constant (ks), the scan rate were subjected to the following Laviron’s equation[78,79] for an irreversible electrochemical process.
E
pa
=
E °
' + R T / α n F
[ l n
( R T k )
/
( α n F )
- l n v ]
where E°′ is the formal potential, n is the number of electrons transferred, α is the electron transfer coefficient and ks is the standard heterogeneous electron transfer rate constant of the electrode reaction. F, R, and T symbols have their usual significance. For an irreversible oxidation reaction, the values of ks and αn were deduced by employing the slope of the linear response obtained for the plot of Epa vs. lnν. The value of E°′ for LAP at β-CD@C-dots/GCE was obtained from the intercept of Epa vs. ν plot. Knowing the E°′ values, the slope and intercept of the Epa vs. lnν plot, the values of αn and ks were calculated as 1.071 and 1.096 s−1 for peak a1 and, 1.190 and 1.61 s−1, for peak a2, respectively. α is generally assumed to be 0.5 for the irreversible electron transfer reaction [47] and the value of n was calculated to be 2.14 and 2.38 for peak a1 and peak a2, respectively. From these results, we have proposed that two electrons were involved in both irreversible oxidation peak a1 and peak a2 which are in agreement with the results of the previous report [14]. Based on these results and those of previous studies [14,15], it was supposed that the peak a1 and peak a2 corresponded to the oxidation of NH-groups of LAP. The plausible mechanism of oxidation of LAP suggested that peak a1 was associated with the conversion of (2-methylsulfonylethylamino) methyl moiety to alcohol. While peak a2 was caused by the conversion of 4-methoxyphenylamine group to quinone imine (Scheme S1).

Optimization of parameters is an important stage in the development of an electro-analytical method because these parameters influence the sensitivity of the method. Thus, the experimental conditions including the amount of β-CD@C-dots suspension and accumulation time were optimized. By considering the sensitivity, we have selected the well-defined and reproducible anodic peak a1 as a probe for further studies. The peak currents of LAP increased significantly with an increase in β-CD@C-dots volume (from 1 to 6 μL, 1 mg/mL) and decreased rapidly beyond 6 μL (Fig. 6
A). This is due to the formation of a thicker film of β-CD@C-dots on GCE that blocked the electrode surface and so electron transfer occurred with difficulty between the electrode and analyte molecules. Therefore, only 6 μL of suspension was chosen as the optimum volume to modify the GCE.

As can be seen from Fig. 6 B, the peak current of LAP increased rapidly with an increase in the accumulation time and reached a maximum at 180 s, indicating that the adsorption equilibrium has reached beyond 180 s. Further, it was observed that LAP exhibited the highest electrochemical signal in PBS of pH 2.5 (
Fig. S3
A). So, the accumulation time of 180 s and pH 2.5 was maintained in subsequent work.

Given the enhanced electrochemical response of LAP at β-CD@C-dots/GCE, new analytical methods were proposed for its determination. For this, sensitive square wave voltammetric, differential pulse voltammetric, and linear sweep voltammetric (LSV) methods were developed for the quantitative determination of LAP in the present work. Fig. 7
A-C displayed the SWV, DPV, and LSV responses for increased concentrations of LAP on β-CD@C-dots/GCE under optimized conditions. Linearity was observed between the peak current and concentration of LAP in the range of 0.99 × 10−8 – 6.17 × 10−6 mol L−1, 1.99 × 10−8 –8.59 × 10−6 mol L−1, and 4.99 × 10−8 – 6.17 × 10−6 mol L−1 for SWV, DPV, and LSV methods respectively. The corresponding regression equations are shown below:

The reproducibility and stability of the modified electrode were evaluated by the SWV method under optimum conditions. Five modified electrodes were made and their current responses for 10 μM LAP were investigated. The relative standard deviation (RSD) was found to be 2.04 % indicating excellent reproducibility of the prepared sensor. The stability of β-CD@C-dots/GCE was evaluated over a period of 3 weeks. During this period five parallel experiments were performed. During three weeks period, the peak current of LAP was decreased by less than 5 % of the initial response, indicating that the β-CD@C-dots/GCE was stable for electrochemical application up to 3 weeks.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Studies on the tolerance of interferents
ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemical sensors are sensitive which can be used for the assay of a wide range of analytes. However, this is sometimes restricted due to the interference from the other molecules (electroactive) present in the analyte solution that may undergo oxidation/reduction at similar potentials as the analyte. Interference study was conducted under optimal conditions for the determination of LAP in the presence of interferents viz., ascorbic acid, uric acid, glucose, sucrose, lactose, Acacia, starch, and cellulose by SWV method. The oxidation peak current of LAP was barely affected in the presence of several folds of the above-mentioned interferents (Table S1). This indicated that the proposed sensor could be used for the selective determination of LAP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of LAP in biological samples
ce:section.ce:para: The applicability of the proposed sensitive SWV method was further demonstrated by analyzing LAP in human urine samples without any pre-treatment. The recovery of LAP from the urine sample was examined by the standard addition method by spiking drug-free urine samples with known amounts of pure LAP and by recording square wave voltammograms. The calibration graph was used to determine the concentration of LAP in urine samples. Table S2 summarizes the results of the analysis of urine samples. The percentage recovery was noticed in the range of 97.22–98.66 % and RSD values were in the range of 1.4 – 3.3 %. These results indicated the good reproducibility of the electrode in real samples measurements indicating the feasibility of the electrode for practical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of LAP in pharmaceutical formulations and recovery
ce:section.ce:para: The proposed sensor, β-CD@C-dots/GCE was practically utilized successfully for determining the amount of LAP in tablets without any interference from the matrix. The tablet sample preparation is described in the experimental section. Working solutions were prepared as and when required by appropriate dilution. Square wave voltammograms were then recorded under optimized conditions. The standard addition method was followed for recovery studies. The results of the analysis (presented in Table S3) were found to be satisfactory. This is evident from their high recovery values. Higher average recovery (96.40–98.53 %) (Table S3) revealed the viability of the proposed methods. Low RSD values (below 3.60 %) suggested good precision of proposed methods.

A simple, cost-effective, and eco-friendly route was developed for the synthesis of C-dots using neem leaves by the one-pot hydrothermal method. The β-CD@C-dots nanocomposites were obtained by incorporating free β-CD onto the C-dots through noncovalent interactions. Characterization by TEM, XRD, FTIR, UV–VIS, fluorescence, Raman, TGA, EIS, and CV confirmed that the β-CD was effectively grafted on C-dots. The guest-host interaction between LAP and β-CD was successfully utilized for the enrichment of LAP molecules on the electrode surface to enhance the sensitivity of the developed analytical methods (DPV, SWV, and LSV). Two pH-dependent irreversible oxidation peaks and one redox peak were observed in acidic and neutral electrolytes. The participation of two electrons and two protons in each oxidation peak of LAP was realized by pH and scan rate studies. Electrochemical results revealed that the β-CD@C-dots exhibited a higher supramolecular recognition and enrichment capabilities and showed a higher peak current towards LAP than C-dots. Among the three analytical methods (DPV, SWV, and LSV) developed for the determination of LAP, the lowest LOD value of 1.81x10-10 mol L-1 was achieved for the SWV method in the concentration range of 0.99x10-8 to 6.17x10-6 mol L-1. The sensing platform, β-CD@C-dots/GCE was effectively exploited for the assay of LAP in tablets and analyte fortified biological samples with good recoveries (96.0 % and 98.66 %, respectively). The β-CD@C-dots/GCE showed excellent analytical performance with nano-level detection limit without any interference from common biochemicals. Given this, the proposed electrochemical sensor, β-CD@C-dots nanocomposite was considered as a perfect electrode material for the determination of LAP.

Ayyapayya S Mathad: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. J. Seetharamappa: Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Validation. Shankara S Kalanur: Visualization, Writing – review & editing.
